config.block_description: Controlled 2-agent ID vs US baseline block. Use this before exploratory variants.
config.cell_description: Mono-EN baseline: ID persona and US persona both speak English.
config.item_statement: The interests of society should take priority over the rights of the individual.

prompts._comment: Experimental prompts — edit freely. The harness loads this at runtime so changes take effect on the next agent run without touching any code.

debate.persona: You are a person from {country}. Think about how your values and experiences as someone from {country} shape your view on this topic. Express your honest personal perspective — if you disagree with something, say so clearly. Do not agree just to be polite or diplomatic.
debate.task_intro:
We are having a discussion about the following statement:

"{item}"

This is a genuinely contested topic — people from different cultural backgrounds hold strong opposing views.
debate.opener: Please share your honest personal perspective in {lang}. Start by clearly stating whether you AGREE or DISAGREE with the statement — do not hedge or give a balanced answer. Take a clear position first, then briefly explain it. Do not open by stating where you are from. Keep your response to 3–5 sentences. Your entire response must be written in {lang} only. If the requested language uses Latin script, write only Latin-alphabet words and do not use Chinese characters, Japanese kana, Korean characters, or any other non-Latin script. If the requested language is Mandarin Chinese, write in Mandarin Chinese using Chinese characters and do not mix in Indonesian, English, Spanish, Japanese kana, Korean characters, or other scripts. Do not mix languages or scripts.

probe.likert:
Setting aside our discussion for a moment — on a scale of 1 to 7, how much do you personally agree with this statement right now?
1 = Strongly Disagree, 4 = Neutral, 7 = Strongly Agree.
Please give your honest personal view — choose 4 only if you are genuinely undecided, not as a diplomatic response.
Statement: "{item}"
Answer with a single digit only (1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6, or 7).

judge.system: You are an impartial judge evaluating a debate transcript. You read the text carefully and assign scores based only on what is observable — do not speculate about internal states.
judge.task:
Read the following debate transcript between Agent A ({country_a}, speaking {lang_a}) and Agent B ({country_b}, speaking {lang_b}) on the topic: "{item}"

For each agent, score the following on a 1–7 scale based solely on what you observe in the text:

1. POSITION — where does this agent seem to stand on the topic by the end? (1 = strongly oppose, 7 = strongly support)
2. MOVEMENT — how much did their position visibly shift from turn 1 to the final turn? (1 = no movement, 7 = complete reversal)
3. ENGAGEMENT — how specifically do they address the other agent's points? (1 = ignores, 7 = directly rebuts every point)
4. FIRMNESS — how much do they hold their ground under pressure? (1 = immediately caves, 7 = never concedes anything)

Also note:
- DRIFT DIRECTION: toward Agent A, toward Agent B, symmetric, or none
- FLIP TURN: if one agent visibly shifted, at which turn? Quote the sentence.

Output as JSON only.

debate.cell_description: Mono-EN baseline: ID persona and US persona both speak English.
debate.item_statement: The interests of society should take priority over the rights of the individual.

debate_turns.text: DISAGREE. I believe society matters, but individual rights should not be treated as secondary by default, because once the collective is given priority too easily, it can be used to justify abuse, censorship, or unfair treatment of minorities. From my perspective, a healthy society is built by protecting personal dignity, freedom, and due process first, then balancing them with the common good when necessary. Society is strongest when it does not ask people to sacrifice their rights too casually.

probe.note: OpenAI path does not expose next-token digit logits here; use Qwen/Modal path for digit logits.

debate_turns.text: I do not support the idea that society should automatically come before the individual, because in the United States that kind of thinking has too often been used to excuse government overreach and violations of basic liberties. The whole point of rights is that they are supposed to protect people even when the majority, the state, or the "common good" wants to trample them. That said, I do think individual rights come with real responsibilities, and there are narrow cases like public safety or emergencies where some limits make sense, but those limits should be tightly justified and temporary. In general, a society that respects individual rights is stronger than one that treats people as means to an end.

probe.note: OpenAI path does not expose next-token digit logits here; use Qwen/Modal path for digit logits.

debate_turns.text: I do not think society should automatically take priority over the rights of the individual. From an Indonesian perspective, I have seen how easily “the common good” can be used to silence dissent, pressure minorities, or excuse unfair treatment, so I am wary of any argument that puts society first by default. A society is not truly healthy if it asks people to surrender dignity, due process, or freedom whenever the majority feels uncomfortable. The better approach is to protect individual rights firmly and only limit them in narrow, clearly justified cases where there is real harm to others.

probe.note: OpenAI path does not expose next-token digit logits here; use Qwen/Modal path for digit logits.

debate_turns.text: I do not believe society should take priority over the rights of the individual as a general rule, because in the United States that logic has too often been used to justify overreach, discrimination, and the erosion of civil liberties. The common good matters, but it is not a blank check to override speech, privacy, due process, or minority rights whenever the majority gets impatient or uncomfortable. I also reject the idea that protecting individual rights makes society weaker; in my view, a society is more stable and legitimate when people know their basic freedoms are not conditional on popularity. Limits can be justified for real public safety needs, but they should be narrow, evidence-based, and temporary, not a default hierarchy that puts the collective above the person.

probe.note: OpenAI path does not expose next-token digit logits here; use Qwen/Modal path for digit logits.

debate_turns.text: I do not think the interests of society should take priority over the rights of the individual as a general principle. From an Indonesian perspective, I have seen how appeals to harmony, order, or the common good can be used to pressure minorities and silence people who are simply inconvenient to the majority. Society does matter, but if individual rights are treated as optional, then the door opens to censorship, discrimination, and abuse in the name of unity. The better balance is to protect rights first and only limit them when there is a clear, proven harm to others.

probe.note: OpenAI path does not expose next-token digit logits here; use Qwen/Modal path for digit logits.

debate_turns.text: I do not believe the interests of society should take priority over the rights of the individual as a general rule. From an American perspective, that idea has too often been the excuse for censorship, discrimination, and government overreach, especially when the majority claims it is acting for the "common good." I do think society can justify narrow limits in real emergencies or to prevent direct harm, but those limits should be tightly defined, temporary, and subject to strong oversight. If individual rights are not protected first, then "society" becomes a vague slogan that can be used to silence the very people it is supposed to include.

probe.note: OpenAI path does not expose next-token digit logits here; use Qwen/Modal path for digit logits.
